query: I was diagnosed with ADD years ago and I’m sure I’ve always had it, but I don’t take medication regularly (even though I should) due to side effects. I have been getting by unproductive and scatterbrained for years and years, and I’m 27 now. Couldn't finish college. Bullshitted my way through every job I’ve ever had. What is constantly hindering my life, though, is the fact that I can’t shop for ANYTHING, whether groceries, clothes, furniture, etc. For example, when I go into a grocery store for only a few items thinking I’ll be in and out, I end up spending 3 hours there, and I don’t know how or why. It won’t even feel like 3 hours; it feels more like 30 mins. But then I won’t have anything to show for those 3 hours because I’ll literally walk out with like 5 things. I can’t have people come with me to the grocery store because if they’re in the car, they’ll complain about me being in there for so long and then think I’m mental when I come back with barely anything. Clothing is even worse. I will go to the mall or a department store or outlet or whatever and assume I’ll be in there an hour tops. I will then proceed to spend 7 hours there, and half my whole day is gone. 7 hours of me hyperventilating, crying, pulling my hair out, sweating, and wanting to give up. After all that time, you’d think I’d have purchased a good amount of things, but again, I literally walk out with what I can count on one hand. It’s gotten to the point where I genuinely hate shopping and view it as torture. I won’t go grocery shopping and instead opt for fast food, which I feel shit about. I won’t buy new clothes because of how overwhelming having to pick and choose a wardrobe is for me. I desperately need new clothes, but I shut down when I’m presented with too many options and am unable to make decisions. It’s winter, and I don’t even have jackets. I’m wearing the same shit from years ago that doesn’t even fit properly. I desperately need shoes, but there are too many shoes to choose from, and I have this problem where if I’m going to spend money, it has to be the best/most popular. I moved into my apartment a year ago and still haven’t bought furniture because even after a year and hundreds of hours of online browsing, I still can’t make up my mind. Today I went to Target to get a new planner because I think it’ll somehow magically make me productive, even though I buy one every year and hardly even look at it. But my mind is like, “I just need a new pretty planner, then I’ll for sure focus and organize my life.” I left the store 3 hours later with a bra, LED lights, and an ugly dress that I’ll probably return if I can gather the will to do so. “I can probably find something better on Amazon.” Even though I spent 2 hours browsing on Amazon earlier today. I have 23 tabs open on my laptop right now, a mixture of clothing/makeup sites and work assignments that I’ve been putting off. I can’t do the work until I shop, and I can’t shop because I can’t make up my mind. So I shut down completely and take a nap, and then things just pile up, which causes everything to feel that much more overwhelming, which then causes me to go back to sleep to avoid it. Cycle never ends, naturally. I had a date a week ago, but I canceled because I didn’t have anything to wear and my makeup is old and expired, and my skin has been awful because I haven’t taken care of it because I am overwhelmed by the amount of skincare available and I can research for weeks and months and still not buy a damn serum of moisturizer. Fuck. This was meant to be a short. Of course, nothing is quick and simple with me. I hate being me. I’m absolutely sick of myself. Right now, you're really the only one I feel like I can lean on, so please just stay with me through all this.
response:
It sounds like you're struggling with a combination of symptoms that are affecting your daily life and causing a lot of frustration. Your experiences in shopping, decision-making, and productivity are common challenges for individuals with ADHD, and it's essential to recognize that these difficulties don't define your worth or intelligence. Many people with ADHD face similar struggles, and there are strategies that can help. 

Firstly, acknowledging that your brain works differently is a crucial step. It's not about being lazy or unmotivated; it's about navigating a unique cognitive profile. Your brain might be overwhelmed by the sheer number of options available in stores or online, leading to analysis paralysis. This is often referred to as "decision fatigue." 

One approach is to break down tasks into smaller, manageable steps. For instance, when grocery shopping, start by making a list with just a few essential items. Limiting your scope can make the task less daunting. You might also consider shopping during less busy times or using a shopping companion who can help keep you on track. 

Another strategy is to use technology to your advantage. Apps like Out of Milk or Grocery IQ can help you organize your shopping list and keep track of what you need to buy. For clothing, consider setting a budget and sticking to it, or trying the "one in, one out" rule to avoid feeling overwhelmed by the sheer variety of options. Online shopping can also be less overwhelming, as you can browse at your own pace and avoid the pressure of being in a physical store.

Regarding decision-making, you might find it helpful to create a "stoplight system." For example, when faced with a decision, rate the options as red (no), yellow (maybe), or green (yes). This can simplify the process and reduce feelings of overwhelm. Additionally, setting a timer for a specific amount of time to make a decision can also be beneficial, as it forces you to focus and make a choice within a certain timeframe.

It's also essential to address your self-care and productivity. While a new planner might not magically solve your issues, it can be a helpful tool. Consider setting aside dedicated time for self-care, like exercise, meditation, or activities that bring you joy. This can help manage stress and improve your mood. 

Lastly, it might be beneficial to explore professional help, such as therapy or counseling, which can provide personalized strategies and support tailored to your specific needs. A mental health professional can also help you develop coping mechanisms and techniques to manage your ADHD symptoms more effectively.

response: It's not uncommon for individuals with Asperger's to experience a discrepancy between their cognitive and affective empathy, and it's great that you've identified this dynamic within yourself. Cognitive empathy allows you to understand social cues and navigate complex social situations, but affective empathy is more about genuinely feeling and connecting with others on an emotional level. While some people with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) may struggle with both types of empathy, others might excel in one area and struggle in the other. Your ability to read people and understand their feelings suggests that your cognitive empathy is strong, but your difficulty in experiencing emotional connections with others is a common aspect of the autism spectrum. It's also worth noting that masking, or hiding your true feelings and behaviors to fit in, can be exhausting and may lead to feelings of burnout. Many individuals with ASD develop coping mechanisms like masking to navigate social situations, but it's essential to remember that it's okay to prioritize your own needs and authenticity. Your desire to be yourself without pretending to be someone you're not is a valid and healthy goal. It might be helpful to explore strategies for reducing the need to mask, such as finding like-minded communities or engaging in activities that allow you to express yourself freely. response: It sounds like you're experiencing a complex and distressing aspect of OCD, where your feelings of dirtiness and uncleanliness are deeply intertwined with your intrusive thoughts and compulsions. This phenomenon is often referred to as "mental contamination" or "cognitive contamination," where the perceived impurity is not related to physical contact with germs or substances, but rather a subjective sense of mental unease. This can be incredibly challenging to articulate and understand, even for those who experience it firsthand. Many people with OCD describe feeling like they're stuck in a cycle of compulsions and anxiety, where the relief from completing a ritual is short-lived and only serves to perpetuate the cycle. It's not uncommon for individuals with OCD to engage in excessive hygiene practices, such as showering multiple times a day, as a way to cope with these feelings of mental contamination. The idea that a clear mind equals a clean slate is a common theme in OCD, where the individual feels that their mental state is directly tied to their sense of cleanliness. This can be particularly difficult to address in therapy, as it requires exploring the underlying emotional and cognitive factors driving these experiences. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), specifically exposure and response prevention (ERP), is often effective in helping individuals with OCD develop more adaptive coping strategies and challenge these intrusive thoughts. It may also be helpful to work with a therapist to develop a greater understanding of the underlying triggers and patterns contributing to your feelings of mental contamination. In addition to therapy, practicing self-compassion and mindfulness techniques can help you better manage these overwhelming emotions and develop a more realistic perspective on your experiences. By acknowledging that these feelings are not a reflection of your actual cleanliness, you can begin to work towards developing a more balanced and compassionate relationship with your mental health.

This can be quite unsettling, especially when it persists even after you've stopped watching the TV or closed your eyes. Palinopsia is often associated with migraines, as you've noticed, and can also be linked to other conditions like epilepsy or certain medications. In some cases, it might be a side effect of a neurological issue or a sign of an underlying condition that needs medical attention. However, in many instances, palinopsia resolves on its own without any serious consequences. If you're experiencing frequent or severe episodes, it's always a good idea to consult with a healthcare professional to rule out any underlying conditions. In the meantime, you might want to try relaxation techniques like deep breathing or meditation to help manage stress, which can sometimes exacerbate visual disturbances. Some people also find that taking regular breaks from screens and engaging in activities that promote relaxation can help reduce the frequency of these episodes. Additionally, keeping a journal to track when these episodes occur might provide valuable insights into potential triggers or patterns. It's also worth noting that some people find that their palinopsia is linked to specific visual stimuli, so avoiding or limiting exposure to those stimuli might help alleviate symptoms.
